---
title: "Exercise: a Box<dyn> heterogeneous collection"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Build a `Vec<Box<dyn BioRecord>>` (a vector whose elements can each be a different concrete type, all sharing the `BioRecord` trait) holding mixed FASTA, FASTQ, and BED records, then iterate over it and print a summary line per record.

## Background

The natural-looking type `Vec<dyn BioRecord>` does **not** compile. A [`Vec<T>`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/vec/struct.Vec.html) stores its elements contiguously in memory, so the compiler needs to know `sizeof(T)` at compile time. A [trait object](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch17-02-trait-objects.html) `dyn BioRecord` (gloss: a value whose concrete type is only known at run time, accessed through the trait) is **unsized** — a `FastqRecord` is bigger than a `BedRecord`, so `dyn BioRecord` has no fixed size.

The fix is to put each record behind a [`Box`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/boxed/struct.Box.html) — a heap allocation with a fixed-size pointer (gloss: a single machine word) on the stack. Now every element of the vector is the same size: one `Box`. The actual records live on the heap, possibly of wildly different sizes.

When you call `r.name()` on a `Box<dyn BioRecord>`, Rust performs **dynamic dispatch** (gloss: the runtime looks up the right method via a small per-type table called a *vtable*, instead of baking the call into the binary at compile time). It is slightly slower than a direct call, but the cost is usually negligible compared to whatever the method itself does.

## The exercise

Open the starter project:

```bash
cd day8/ex-trait-objects
```

You will see a single Rust source file at `src/main.rs`. It already contains:

- A trait `BioRecord` with two methods: `name(&self) -> &str` and `length(&self) -> usize`
- Three structs — `FastaRecord`, `FastqRecord`, `BedRecord` — each with an `impl BioRecord` block whose method bodies are `// TODO`
- A `summarize` function that takes a `&[Box<dyn BioRecord>]` and returns one summary line per record (this one is **provided** — do not modify it)
- A `main` that builds a heterogeneous `Vec<Box<dyn BioRecord>>` and prints the summary
- A `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block with six unit tests

Your job: fill in the six TODOs (two per struct) so all tests pass. Each one is a single-line method body.

You may **not** change the trait, the struct definitions, the tests, `main`, or `summarize`.

## How to run

From inside `day8/ex-trait-objects/`:

```bash
cargo build           # compile
cargo test            # run the tests — these should be failing at first
cargo run             # see the demo output
```

When all six tests pass, `cargo test` will print:

```
test result: ok. 6 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured
```

and `cargo run` should print:

```
chr1 (12 bp)
read42 (8 bp)
chr1 (100 bp)
```

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — `name()`: return a `String` field as a `&str`"}
All three `name()` methods are one-liners that return a borrowed view of a `String` field on `self`.

```rust
fn name(&self) -> &str {
    &self.name      // for FastaRecord and FastqRecord
}
```

For `BedRecord` the field is called `chrom` instead:

```rust
fn name(&self) -> &str {
    &self.chrom
}
```

The `&self.<field>` reborrows the [`String`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/string/struct.String.html) field as a [`&str`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.str.html) (string slice) — Rust's [deref coercion](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch15-02-deref.html#implicit-deref-coercions-with-functions-and-methods) does the `String` → `&str` conversion for you.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — `length()` for `FastaRecord` and `FastqRecord`"}
For the two sequence-based records, the length is just the length of the `sequence` field:

```rust
fn length(&self) -> usize {
    self.sequence.len()
}
```

[`String::len`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/string/struct.String.html#method.len) returns a [`usize`](https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/primitive.usize.html), which matches the trait's return type — no cast needed.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — `length()` for `BedRecord` (with a type cast)"}
`BedRecord` doesn't carry a sequence — its length is `end - start`. The catch is that `start` and `end` are `u64`, but the trait says `length` returns `usize`. Convert with an [`as` cast](https://doc.rust-lang.org/reference/expressions/operator-expr.html#type-cast-expressions):

```rust
fn length(&self) -> usize {
    (self.end - self.start) as usize
}
```

On a 64-bit machine `usize` and `u64` are the same width, so the cast is a no-op at runtime; the compiler just needs it to satisfy the type signature.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Solution"}
The full reference implementation lives in `day8/ex-trait-objects-solution/src/main.rs`. Try not to peek until your own version passes all six tests.
:::

## Where this leads

- **Day 4 uses [`noodles`](https://crates.io/crates/noodles) heavily**, and `noodles` uses traits everywhere — for readers, writers, records, indices. You will use them without thinking about them again; this exercise is the last time you have to wire one up by hand.
- **`Box<dyn Error>` is the most common `Box<dyn>` you will see in real Rust.** It is the standard return-type trick for "this function can fail in several different ways and I do not want to enumerate them yet": `fn foo() -> Result<T, Box<dyn std::error::Error>>`. The Day 4 lecture introduces it alongside the [`?` operator](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/ch09-02-recoverable-errors-with-result.html#a-shortcut-for-propagating-errors-the--operator).
- **Generic alternatives.** When all elements of a collection have the **same** concrete type, prefer a generic `Vec<T>` over `Vec<Box<dyn Trait>>` — it is faster (no vtable lookup) and lets the compiler inline more aggressively. Reach for `Box<dyn Trait>` when the elements really are heterogeneous, as in this exercise.
